theorem: For every S in C* and every machinery-closed R in E*, if P*(S,R) holds and the system admits commitment-changing transitions, then R exposes an effective, history-sensitive admissibility relation that distinguishes permitted from forbidden commitment evolution and is invariant under frozen commitment equivalence.
proof_strategy: recover successive commitment states through the registered faithful interface
recover transition admissibility answers and their governing constraints
construct the admissibility relation from those total registered answers
show that denial of the relation contradicts operational, historical, dependency, or query-totality preservation
quotient only by the frozen bidirectional commitment-equivalence relation
anti_trivialization_rules: mere state change is not constrained evolution
post hoc acceptance of every observed transition is not a constraint
final-output equality does not preserve evolution constraints
an always-true admissibility predicate is insufficient when forbidden transitions exist
an always-false predicate is insufficient when permitted transitions exist
unrecorded external policy machinery is prohibited
future-only reconstruction cannot replace historical admissibility
failure may not be collapsed into Unknown
nonclaims: unrestricted metaphysical laws of rational change
